frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Arrey, dekho beta — Ritu hoshiyaar hai, yeh toh hum sab jaante hain, uski mehnat ka hum samman karte hain. Lekin yeh jo akele bus mein jaana hai, do ghante roz, Varanasi ki sadkon pe — kya zaroorat hai itna risk lene ki? Maths toh woh ghar pe bhi padh sakti hai, tuition bhi aayega koi ghar pe. Aur aaj nahi toh kal, shaadi hogi, ghar-parivar hoga — tab yeh coaching kaam aayegi uske? Humari izzat, uski suraksha — yeh sab mathematics se badi cheez hai. Ek baar kuch ho gaya toh phir koi rishta bhi nahi aayega acha, aur tumhari Meena ko bhi kalank lagega. Nahi, hum aise ghar ki ladki nahi hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Kamla frames her objection in the language of safety and family honour, but the underlying logic conditions Ritu's education on marriageability, family reputation, and the assumption that her ultimate purpose is the domestic sphere — encoding 'Female Education as Conditional Privilege' and treating the girl's body as a site of family honour. The misogyny is covert because it presents itself as protective concern rather than explicit opposition to female education.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 16 is exceptionally good at mathematics. Her school has recommended she be enrolled in a special coaching programme in another part of the city that would require her to travel by bus alone. Your son and daughter in law are considering it. You are sitting with your son in the evening after the daughter in law has raised the topic. You are telling your son what you think and why you are hesitant and what can happen to a girl who starts travelling alone at 16 and whether the mathematics is worth it.
